title: Flipkart Merge Sort Orders
description: Flipkart's supply chain dashboard receives a list of order amounts in arbitrary order. The team wants a stable, predictable O(n log n) sort using classic merge sort (divide the array, sort each half, merge). Given `orders` (array of integers, possibly with duplicates and negatives), return a new array sorted ascending using merge sort. Built-in sort is not allowed — write the divide-and-conquer recursion yourself.
hints: Base case: length <= 1 already sorted.
Mid se split kar, dono halves recursively sort kar, fir do-pointer merge.
Merge step me extra array allocate kar — in-place merge tough hai.

title: Swiggy Power of X Recursive Halving
description: Swiggy's pricing engine needs `x^n` for surge multipliers (x is a real number, n is an integer that can be negative). Implement `myPow(x, n)` using **recursive halving** (NOT Math.pow, NOT a linear loop). For negative n, compute the positive power then invert.
hints: x^n = (x^(n/2))^2 when n even; x · x^(n-1) when n odd.
n negative ho toh helper(x, -n) call karke 1/result return kar — but n = INT_MIN ko BigInt-safe handle kar.
Recursion depth ~log2(|n|), safe for n up to 2^31.

title: Cricket Tournament Min Max Comparisons
description: A cricket inning sub-tournament has `n` players each with a power score. The selectors want both the minimum and maximum scores using a **tournament-style divide & conquer** that performs at most ~3n/2 comparisons total (better than the naive 2(n-1)). Given `scores`, return `[min, max]`.
hints: Pair up adjacent elements, compare them once, then push the smaller into 'mins' and larger into 'maxes' — saves comparisons.
Recursive variant: split, get [min1,max1] and [min2,max2], combine with 2 comparisons.
Base cases: length 1 returns [a,a]; length 2 returns sorted pair.

title: Flipkart Quickselect Kth Largest Order
description: Flipkart wants the k-th largest order amount from today's stream. Use **Quickselect** (the divide & conquer cousin of quicksort): partition around a pivot and recurse only into the side that contains the answer. Given `orders` and `k` (1-indexed: k=1 means largest), return the k-th largest value. Expected average O(n).
hints: Convert k-th largest into k-th smallest by using index = n - k (0-indexed target).
Partition (Lomuto or Hoare): pick pivot, place it in its final sorted position; recurse only into the side containing target.
Random pivot or median-of-three se O(n^2) worst case avoid hota hai.

title: Swiggy Zone Max Subarray Divide Conquer
description: Swiggy splits a delivery zone into bisected sub-zones whose hourly profit/loss values form an array. Find the **maximum contiguous subarray sum** using the **divide & conquer** approach (Bentley's algorithm) — distinct from Kadane's DP. The answer is the max of: best sum in left half, best sum in right half, or best sum that crosses the midpoint.

examples.explanation: Single element is allowed; pick the largest.

hints: Recursion: maxSub(lo, hi) = max(maxSub(left), maxSub(right), maxCross(left,mid,right)).
maxCross: from mid going left, expand and track max prefix; from mid+1 going right, track max suffix; sum them.
Kadane mat use kar — purpose hai D&C ka pattern dikhana.

title: Razorpay Count Inversions
description: Razorpay's settlement audit defines an *inversion* as a pair (i, j) with i < j but `txn[i] > txn[j]` — a possibly out-of-order pair. Count the total number of inversions in O(n log n) using the **merge-sort variant**.
hints: Standard merge sort, but during merge, when right element is taken before left, it forms inversions with all remaining left items.
Increment count by (mid - i + 1) at that moment.
Don't mutate the input — clone before recursing.

title: PhonePe Count Smaller After Self
description: For each transaction in PhonePe's daily log, count how many later transactions have a strictly smaller amount. Given `txn`, return an array `result` where `result[i]` = number of indices j > i with `txn[j] < txn[i]`. Required complexity: O(n log n) using **merge sort with index tracking**.
hints: Sort an array of indices, not values. During merge, when a right index is placed before a left index, add (rightTaken count so far) to result[leftIndex].
Equivalent statement: this is inversion counting per element, decomposed by left index.
Track how many right-half elements have already been merged when left element gets consumed.

title: Amazon Reverse Pairs
description: An Amazon analytics team defines a *reverse pair* as a pair (i, j) with i < j and `nums[i] > 2 * nums[j]`. Count the total number of reverse pairs. Required complexity: O(n log n) via the **merge-sort variant**.
hints: After both halves are sorted, count cross-pairs in O(n) with a two-pointer scan: for each i in left, advance j in right while nums[i] > 2*nums[j].
Do the COUNTING pass before the merge step; sorting and counting use disjoint comparisons (>2x vs > or <=).
Use 2*nums[j] cast to a wider type if needed to avoid overflow.

title: Razorpay Count Range Sum
description: Razorpay wants to count how many contiguous transaction subarrays of `txn` have a sum lying in `[lower, upper]` (inclusive). Required complexity: O(n log n) using **merge sort over prefix sums**.
hints: Build prefix sums P[0..n] with P[0] = 0. Count pairs (i, j), i < j, with lower <= P[j] - P[i] <= upper.
Merge sort P. During merge step, both halves are sorted — for each i in left, find the window of valid j in right with two pointers.
Count first, then merge — exactly like the reverse-pairs trick.

title: Cricket Different Ways To Add Parentheses
description: A cricket sub-tournament scoring formula is given as a string of digits and operators (`+`, `-`, `*`). For every way to parenthesize the formula, compute its value, and return all possible results in any order. Solve using **operator-as-split-point divide & conquer**: each operator partitions the expression into a left and right sub-expression.
constraints: Numbers fit in safe integer range
hints: For each operator at index i: recursively get all results from expr[0..i-1] and expr[i+1..]; combine pairwise.
Base case: if no operator in substring, return [parseInt(substring)].
Memoize by substring to skip exponential recomputation.

title: Swiggy Median Of Two Sorted Delivery Streams
description: Two Swiggy zones publish delivery times sorted ascending into `a` and `b`. Find the **median** of the combined stream in **O(log(min(m, n)))** using divide & conquer (binary search the partition).
hints: Binary search on the smaller array. Choose i in [0, m]; set j = (m+n+1)/2 - i.
Valid partition: a[i-1] <= b[j] AND b[j-1] <= a[i].
Use sentinels -Infinity/+Infinity for out-of-range indices.

title: Flipkart Kth Smallest In Two Sorted Arrays
description: Flipkart's price feed gives two sorted arrays `a` and `b`. Find the **k-th smallest** element (1-indexed) of the combined collection in **O(log(m+n))** using divide & conquer — discard ~k/2 elements per step.
hints: Compare a[i + k/2 - 1] with b[j + k/2 - 1]; the smaller's first half can NEVER contain the answer — drop it and reduce k.
Handle empty arrays by directly indexing into the other.
Stop when k = 1: return min of the two front elements.

title: Flipkart Skyline Of Warehouse Buildings
description: Flipkart's warehouse complex has rectangular buildings given as `[left, right, height]`. Compute the **skyline** silhouette — list of `[x, y]` key points where the visible roofline changes. Solve with **divide & conquer**: split buildings into halves, compute each silhouette recursively, then merge two silhouettes (line-sweep style).
hints: Split into halves, recurse, then merge two skylines like merge sort.
Merge: walk both with two pointers, tracking heights h1, h2; emit a key point whenever max(h1, h2) changes; skip duplicate y values.
